author: @marieellis6084
text:
They need ideas like this in places like Pakistan where electricity is some places is spotty at best if not almost non existent. Literally just 1-2 hours a day in some villages. People still use wood coal to heat their homes, and it's burned in an open container filled with sand.. even though they may burn it outside until it's not visible smoking anymore, it still smokes up the room. It's a fight between being cold and suffocating. This would be a way better solution. Far less vapors should come off of a clean burning alcohol and that is something available in Pakistan, although I do not know the price difference between coal & it and that would make all the difference in the end. Open flames aren't really the problem. pakistani people deal with dangerous things on a daily basis in their lives. Sometimes just plugging something electric is dangerous bc there no plug 🔌 it just two bare wires they stick into the electric sockets 🙃 as well as their gas burning heaters throw off 🔥 anyway. 
But I love ideas like this for places that truly could need it to help the people.

author: @steelo8520
text: I added a "catalytic copper wick ", I found by putting an upright tube of copper mesh on the safety screen (scaled like a Bday cake) after it heated up it attracted the flame and changed the shape and colour of it to like a really really deep gloomy orange lantern light plus when the flame is blown out the copper/ethanol catalytic reaction kept it glowing and  put it into a standby/economy heat mode . So for times say when your solo camping in the cold or in a car for the winter, yep, no flame.....

author: @mikefozard4057
text:
Inspiring video as always! 
I made a close approximation of this, except my reservoir section was filled with ceramic fibre topped with a disc of carbon felt then the stainless mesh. The initial burn was strong but controlled, presumably by the mesh. It then died down to a low blue flame for the majority of the rest of the burn. Not ideal for my use case (a reasonably decorative room heater), perhaps my can was too tall and narrow for efficient wicking. Love the safety flame concept but the search goes on for my consistent controlled burn.

author: @Tailspin80
text: It’s all a bit dangerous but useful in an emergency. CO and CO2 are a potential danger, plus particulates in the air if you burn the wrong fuel. Then there is the risk of setting fire to the curtains, the open 5l bottle of alcohol etc! We heat with a properly installed 8kW wood burner which provides enough heat so we won’t be using the central heating this winter. With an air inlet and sealed flue none of the nasties get into the air we breathe. Obviously this doesn’t work for everyone- you need a free/cheap source for the wood, but it works for us.

author: @grandfunkrailroad89
text: This doesn't exactly pertain to this exact invention, but more on the electrical generation side of things and storage. Have you ever heard of John Hutchinson? And his Discovery called the Hutchinson effect? He basically found out a way to make miniature self generating power cells that can have up to 1.5 volts each and they're made out of quarter inch copper tubing about an inch long and either a galvanized nail or magnesium rod of some sort small enough to fit inside the copper tubing without touching it. He uses a mixture of Epsom salt, Rochelle salt, a little iron pyrite, Galena, and calcium carbonate and it creates a self regenerating power medium between the nail and the copper tubing. I think you could really go somewhere with his discoveries because he's gone basically missing from the whole public scene since I seen his first video on it which I can't even find anymore. I have a recipe that I wrote down though from his video if you would like it as a starter

author: @artifactingreality
text: The chemical name for floral foam is EXPANDED THERMOSET POLYMER. Floral foam is made of phenol, formaldehyde polymers, surfactants, and wetting agents. Floral foam can cause eye, skin, and respiratory tract irritation.

author: @nunyabiznez8120
text: PLEASE do NOT weld galvanized steel.  A lot of strapping is galvanized.  Welding the coating vaporizes the zinc and the fumes are toxic.  There are special procedures to use if you must weld without removing the coating with a wire wheel or grinder.

author: @mfbfreak
text: 1:55 be aware that Oasis is essentialy foamy Bakelite without a filler, it's phenol-formaldehyde foam. Make sure that it cannot get to the decomposition temperature, or it can release formaldehyde vapor.

replies.author: @BurntFossil
replies.text: Do you know what the decomposition temp is?

replies.author: @mfbfreak
replies.text: @BurntFossil Phenol formaldehyde resin begins to decompose around 300 degrees C. I do not know if/how much it offgases at lower temperatures.

author: @Buzzhumma
text: Very nice indeed. I was wondering how they blend ethanol into petrol with the idea of blending some more hydrogen atoms into my used motor oil to change its density so as to modify its burning properties . Breaking down the alkanes is the idea using the heat of the fire in the process to break down the longer chains . Not sure if it's  possible on the fly but I did add some sodium hydroxide to some dish soap and mixed it in the oil and not sure if it worked but it did seem to cool down the flame which inturn vapourises less and that may have just been the dish soap so that might be enough for now . The other way I have been doing this to create a soot less fire is by adding fibrous bark from a tree to change the surface area of the fuels so it can handle the air ratio better . Any suggestions Rob or anybody else ?

author: @JamesWilson-pq9qp
text: Humphrey Davy safety screen is a technology used to make all electrical equipment explosion proof.  Originally used in mines  for lamps; going back to an 1815 invention.  It really is unbelievable how well it works  -  excellent demonstration  - Rob!

author: @lagunafishing
text:
Anyone tried mixing a small amount of water with the alcohol?
I've not tried it yet, but you should end up with a cooler flame that burns for longer. The same idea works with hydrogen pen torches where alcohol is added to water to cool the flame.

replies.author: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN
replies.text: I tried that a few weeks back, the more water that I added, the smaller the flame got and the longer it lasted. The issue is with water, it ends up in the air. Adding moister to the air may not be desirable in some cases, especially if High humidity issues already exists. In a very dry environment this might not be a problem, especially if it's desirable. Some folks use dehumidifiers for certain situations and likewise, some use humidifiers for certain situations.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: most alcohols already have water in them mate - they absorb it from the air

replies.author: @lagunafishing
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yes they do. Methanol was 99.8 percent when bought new 5 years ago (25 litres) but intend adding a bit more water to see if I can cool the flame as I'm burning through 400ml an hour and the heat output is overpowering.

replies.author: @lagunafishing
replies.text: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN Thanks for your reply.

author: @johnpetermullan7333
text: All flames from all flammable materials have what's called a quenching diameter. Since the material itself doesn't burn, only the vapour generated by the heat applied, there is a finite space for  each vapour through which the flame can't pass. Increase or diminish the diameter of the space and you can regulate the flame propagation. The principle is what Mr Davy used to put canaries out of work in coal mines. Maggy Thatcher was probably an admirer.

author: @travismoore7849
text: What if you had a jar with a large diameter metal lid. It uses copper mesh and about five holes in the lid. One in the center for a bolt. On this bolt is two wooden wheels. Holes two holes on the side of the bolt line up with the three hole in each wood wheel. Then two more holes on the outer edge of where the wooded wheels are. The wooden wheels have the copper mesh tacked on to make a cylinder going up to the top of the lid so air can escape. Then two holes in the bottom of the bottom wheel have pipes going to the top of the lid along the outer edge. Inside the jar is a small carbon felt with a steel sheet acting like a spring to hold it against the wall of the jar. In the bottom is your acetone or methanol. The copper sheet should be pre heat treated to make an oxide layer on the outer wall of the mesh. This is done by heating the copper mesh against the bottom of a pot or pan to keep the copper mesh from melting or getting to hot so only the catalytic oxide layer is formed. Once assembled put about a few ounces of acetone in the bottom of the jar. Preheat the mesh a little bit but not too much. Put the lid and assembly back on. If it was made right it should catalyze the acetone and draw fresh air in through the bottom by convection. Though feel free to tell  me how this idea is flawed. Because it is just a planning stage.

author: @jamesross1003
text: I wonder if when the Oasis foam gets low on fuel in it will it then start to melt or burn itself?  A candle wick without wax will burn up.  One thing I have been thinking about doing is to source some of the Oasis and use it to cool carbon arc rods(tightly bound side by side) by filling it with distilled water.  Evaporative cooling.  Then lay on top of them battery carbon rod centers.  Heat the top side, the Oasis cools the bottom side and see if that will produce current.  I figure if the top layer of rods has a different impedance than the bottom rods then it may make a thermopile much like your paper and pencil one only larger.  I don't know, but may be worth a try to find out as it would not be quite as fragile.

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text:
I was just thinking of tuna cans!  Maybe making a few holes in the top with one of those triangular shaped punches on the other end of a can opener we used to use to open up the big cans of pineapple juice.  Içve used those forever and I still have no idea what those things are called!

I saw another guy on YouTube bend the triangle bits all the way in.  Not only were they air holes, but they formed a little raised stand to set the pot on above the flame.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@colleenforrest7936
 
The ones that had the round end on one side and the pointed end on the other we called bottle openers.

The one's with the crank we called can openers.
And since the pointed tool is used to open cans, it's a can opener.

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text: @scotttovey the rounded ones open the bottles and the crank can openers open that cans, but the pointy ones put the triangle holes in the lids so you can pour liquid out.  Just wondering if those had an official name other than just calling those can openers too

author: @Techno_Nomadic
text: I'll have to ask: If the fuel cell is offgassing due to the heat from the flame, and the top part is acting as a flame arrestor, thus lifting it way takes the ignition away from the fuel, is not the fuel cell still offgassing volatile vapors? IE, if for whatever reason a spark remains, or another nearby source of ignition exists, can the fuel cell not directly maintain a flame without the presence of the burner head? I think that the burner head isn't necessary for there to be a flame, and if it isn't knocked off quickly and cleanly, such that some of the fuel cell's mesh is exposed, it will ignite directly on the cell.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: check this out https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gTR6yiPW3OA

replies.author: @Techno_Nomadic
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Exactly as I thought. Once warm enough to offgas above the flash point it will simply continue to burn. However, alcohol fuels can be diluted with water and immediately puts out the fire, which is the primary reason they are used in marine applications.

author: @AB-C1
text:
Great video and idea!! Well done!
Just a thought would this be safe INDOORS!? (Given its burning alcohol that's ALREADY vapourised I can't imagine this giving off much in the way of Carbon monoxide if anything at all? Given its a clean fuel anyway and it's vapourising before ignition as far as we know!? - Or am I mistaken?) A Carbon monoxide detector reading might be useful to see what levels there are if is safe for indoor use in emergency power outage??  Keep up the fantastic work!
Cheers from London 👍😎🏴󠁧󠁢󠁥󠁮󠁧󠁿🇬🇧

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: I have done it many times with a CO meter and have rarely gotten over 9ppm CO, which is quite acceptable. Just crack a door every couple of hours for a few seconds.

replies.text: well lots of folks do use alcohol burners indoors mate

author: @richbuilds_com
text:
The dust from that oasis stuff is highly carcinogenic (it's comes up a lot in my other hobby: scale model building).

I'd be *very* wary about having it near a heat source / setting it on fire.

Nifty device though. Would that carbon felt work as a reservoir instead?

author: @make425
text:
I think the safety of its okay but I believe that knocking it over while it was on, could still and very possibly and most likely still burn and alcohol and other fuels at elevated temperatures react way differently than first lighting them.  Take ordinary charcoal lighter fluid and heat it then light it and you get a way bigger explosion than the safe charcoal fluid that you normally lighting.
Can we see a kicked over outside test. Not just kicking the top off but knocking the whole container over.

replies.author: @knickly
replies.text: The trangia stoves are filled with fiberglass to prevent spillage, but I think they too continue to burn when tipped over. I've been told that the coil burners self-extinguish when tipped over, though.

author: @maryrobertson5250
text:
Wouldn't the stone wool become a non-burning  'wick' and become a catalyst if your home caught on fire... 
There now, becomes a non burning wick that would become an inferno as the whole house is wickedned.
Only the combustible material: ie: your house burns, while the stone non combustible wool and stone cement materials remain.
Saw a video by Robert Murray Smith in his video The Magical Forever Wick - The Plant Pot Heater On Steroids.
I see so many videos where home builds and van builders using this carbon / lava rock  material but is sounds counter intuitive.

author: @simonhodges6084
text: Speaking of safety do you take carbon monoxide readings of your different heater designs?

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: I can't speak for him, but I have. I use a burner a little bigger than this with methanol and it never measures over 7-9ppm CO. White gas and candles have, especially when they're about to burn out.

replies.author: @simonhodges6084
replies.text: @jerrodlopes186 I was thinking more of the rocket stoves and other wood burning ones.  If it were me, I think it would be prudent to say very loudly and upfront that a particular design is only to be used outdoors.

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: @simonhodges6084 humanity has been burning wood, dung, oil and coal indoors for millenia. I used to work with fireplaces and stoves for a living. Installed or built correctly and used as designed, they are quite safe.

replies.author: @simonhodges6084
replies.text: @jerrodlopes186 I think you are being very naïve here.  We now have many safety regulations regarding such things.  "Installed or built correctly" indeed.  Given a cost of living and energy crisis there is not enough information for desperate folk to do this safely.  Run your argument past your lawyer.  Its no 'defence' at all if someone dies from CO poisoning.

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: @simonhodges6084 and I think you're just the nervous Nelly type. What do I know? I was just an NFPA certified hearth technician.

author: @ScoreGuru123
text: Could you create something that would be like a wood burner in the house, but with no smoke? Basically a cheap alternative to spending £thousands on a wood burner

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: I've been working on it for a long time. All I've come up with is using a small camp stove and running the stovepipe out a window. I used to install the real ones for a living. I've got a wonderful little woodburner that I bought new for less than $300 US. Enough stovepipe and a damper would run you maybe $75-100 more. You'd need to purchase a purpose made fireback and hearth or produce the same with available materials (think stone slabs/concrete wall backing...). There's just no real cheap and safe way to burn wood. PS the trick to not getting smoke is to light a paper log or some such thing and hold it up in the flue for a good thirty seconds before lighting the stove in order to warm up the flue and start a good draft going. Voila! No smoke. Oh, and use only very dry seasoned wood.

author: @PatGilliland
text: Floral foam comes in two main varieties - Oasis - which holds fluid as shown and Sahara which does not. Be sure to get the right one.  Never thought I'd be sharing information I learned in my first job 30+ years ago.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
@ScoreGuru123    Somebody on an older video noted that ceramic fiber works better than carbon felt. Haven't tried it yet, but I have tried carbon felt, and have learned it is definitely not forever, especially with hotter fires/flames (it gets brittle and crumbly faster than one might think, was experimenting with wax burning).  I have some ceramic fiber somewhere, just a matter of finding it.  

  (For alcohols, carbon felt probably is ok and will last a decent amount of time.  Burns cooler)
